metadata.title: Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts

abstract: From the start of data collection for the 2020 US Census, official and celebrity users tweeted about the importance of everyone being counted in the Census and urged followers to complete the questionnaire (so-called social media campaign.) At the same time, social media posts expressing skepticism about the Census became increasingly common. This study distinguishes between different prototypical Twitter user groups and investigates their possible impact on (online) self-completion rate for the 2020 Census, according to Census Bureau data. Using a network analysis method, Community Detection, and a clustering algorithm, Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), three prototypical user groups were identified: "Official Government Agency," "Census Advocate," and "Census Skeptic." The prototypical Census Skeptic user was motivated by events about which an influential person had tweeted (e.g., "Republicans in Congress signal Census cannot take extra time to count"). This group became the largest one over the study period. The prototypical Census Advocate was motivated more by official tweets and was more active than the prototypical Census Skeptic. The Official Government Agency user group was the smallest of the three, but their messages-primarily promoting completion of the Censusseemed to have been amplified by Census Advocate, especially celebrities and politicians. We found that the daily size of the Census Advocate user group-but not the other two-predicted the 2020 Census online self-completion rate within five days after a tweet was posted. This finding suggests that the Census social media campaign was successful in promoting completion, apparently due to the help of Census Advocate users who encouraged people to fill out the Census and amplified official tweets. This finding demonstrates that a social media campaign can positively affect public behavior regarding an essential national project like the Decennial Census.

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
paragraphs: In 2020, for the first time, members of the US public were able to self-complete the Decennial Census online. Online, that is, a web survey, completion has several advantages: it can be completed quickly, it is widely accessible, and there is no data entry required (as is needed for selfcompletion by mail) (Couper & Miller, 2008;Tourangeau et al., 2013). Furthermore, during the COVID-19 pandemic period, avoiding personal contact was critical. Making self-completion as easy as possible was a priority which the addition of online to mail self-completion helped advance. Although web surveys are associated with coverage error (e.g., Tourangeau et al., 2013) this is not of similar concern in the Census because, by definition, all residents of the US are enumerated, that is, there is no sampling from a sampling frame (the final completion rate for US 2020 Census is 99.98%.) 1 Also, because the 2020 Census was a mixed mode survey (face-to-face interview, web, and mail), those with limited access to the Internet could be counted via other modes. Nonetheless, the "participation rate" (Tourangeau et al., 2013) is generally lower in web surveys than when data are collected in other modes (Daikeler et al., 2020) so improving the selfcompletion rate (by engaging the public and encouraging response to the Census) 2 is still an important goal for the US Census Bureau. To do so, the Census Bureau studied and monitored the communications environment and sought new/additional opportunities to engage the public and encourage response to the Census (social networking, strategic partnerships, advertising, etc.) 3 However, we have very little knowledge about how well those social engagements on social media platforms like Twitter (now known as X) work and whether they achieve their intended goals. Therefore, in this paper, we propose data-driven strategies to understand how Census data collection can be improved. Furthermore, we aim to connect the power of social media to enhance the richness and robustness of Census data. The knowledge of the effect of governmental message campaigns can also be extended to more general fields and inform social science researchers of new possibilities and strategies to approach social media data.

This study aims to identify "prototypical user groups" on Twitter, who play crucial roles in disseminating Census-related messages. Understanding the dynamics of these groups is essential because they can potentially enhance or undermine efforts to increase Census participation, thus directly impacting the accuracy and completeness of Census data. This research is particularly relevant to a general social science audience as it bridges the gap between digital communication strategies and large-scale civic participation, providing insights that can inform future public information campaigns.

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
sections.header_raw: Current Studies About Census Message Campaign
sections.header_norm: current studies about census message campaign
paragraphs: Two studies have assessed how well attempts to "engage the public and encourage response" work. The first one addressed the effect of strategically mailing online completion invitations to US residents based on Internet coverage information (i.e., the proportion of residents with access to the Internet) (Morrison & Bowen, 2022). And the second study is investigating the effect of Census advertising campaigns (the multilingual advertising we have seen on social media platforms e.g., YouTube and Facebook). However, it is unknown whether the Census's social media message campaign (i.e., tweeting about why the 2020 Census is important) actually boosted the participation rate. Unlike conventional Census advertising campaigns, the effectiveness of the social media message campaign seems likely to depend on the platform. The messages posted by the Census Bureau are relatively unlikely to be read by users on platforms which are meant for connecting with members of one's personal network and sharing more in-depth, personal content (such as Facebook). Twitter, in contrast, facilitates broadcasting messages to everyone, and thus seems to be a better candidate for such a message campaign.

Additionally, unlike advertisements, promotional messages on Twitter about completing the 2020 Census can be spread, discussed, and even argued about by users of the platform. For any given topic, there will be groups of users who agree, disagree, or are indifferent. However, unlike the consumers of traditional advertisements, these users are not just can initiate further rounds of message dissemination. We aim to observe how conversations about the Census message campaign unfold on social media platforms and specifically identify influential user groups whose engagement can significantly impact public response rates.

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
sections.header_raw: Social Media Influence and Census Participation
sections.header_norm: social media influence and census participation
paragraphs: The rationale and mechanism behind a Census social media campaign to improve the participation rate is consistent with findings from web survey research. For example, Wright and Schwager (2008) found that recruiting a well-known authority to author the invitation improved participation in web surveys at the recruitment stage. Similarly, in the Census social media campaign, the message will likely be echoed by politicians (such as former president Barack Obama and then Senator Kamala Harris) as well as celebrities (such as Ariana Grande). Both can be served as wellknown authorities and make the message more convincing. Petrovčič et al. (2016) shows that the online community activities are positively associated with response to a list-based web survey. The same should be true for the 2020 Census even though its web option was not list-based, and the community whose activity we are investigating is Twitter. Once the web survey is live, the participation rate of the 2020 Census should be correlated with public attitudes towards the Census (Fan & Yan, 2010). In this case, the social media message campaign can also partially enhance people's confidence about the 2020 Census. More importantly, based on Dillman's (e.g., 2013) social exchange theory, "the respondent trusts that the expected rewards of responding will outweigh the anticipated costs." The perceived costs of participating in the Census likely include the time and effort to complete the form while the perceived benefits include funding for communities proportional to the number of completed questionnaires. Perceived cost will be reduced by the ease of online response, while perceived benefits) need to be communicated to the public as they may not be self-evident. The latter was emphasized in the Census Bureau's message campaign numerous times. However, as this was the first Decennial Census in which selfcompletion via web survey was an option, the effect of the message campaign was hard to anticipate.

Social media influence relies heavily on user engagement, which can be positive, negative, or without reaction. Understanding how users interact with these messages-whether they support, oppose, or remain neutral-is crucial. This engagement can amplify the reach of the campaign, but it can also introduce challenges, such as the spread of misinformation.

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
paragraphs: This study focuses on Twitter due to its unique structure that facilitates rapid message dissemination across user networks, which may extend the reach of Census campaigns beyond traditional methods. Twitter users form the basic unit of analysis due to the personal nature of posts, which reflect individual opinions and orientations, enabling precise quantification of the influence of different user groups (Mueller, 2015).

Moreover, Twitter's architecture notably supports the broadcast nature of messages from influencers to their followers, enhancing the diffusion of information. This effect is amplified by the tendency of elite users to form connections within their communities of likeminded people, leading to the formation of cohesive groups that persist over time (Wu et al., 2011). These elite sub-networks, comprised of multiple stable communities, are structured and influential and can significantly impact the topics and opinions discussed within them, including important civic activities like the 2020 Census (Motamedi et al., 2020).

However, the platform's susceptibility to political polarization presents a double-edged sword. While it can aid in spreading constructive messages about civic duties such as the Census, it can also propagate skepticism and misinformation, especially from politically motivated elites (Conover et al., 2021). This polarization was particularly evident during the 2016 and 2020 Presidential elections, the latter period overlapping with the administration of 2020 Census, where political elites played a significant role in disseminating misinformation (Mosleh & Rand, 2022). The spread of misinformation can undermine the effectiveness of well-intentioned campaigns by sowing doubt and misinformation among the public. Therefore, the challenge the Census Bureau faced included not only from whether the campaign messages could be successfully spread throughout the network but also how much influence that political misinformation about Census 2020 might have.

These positive and negative engagements on Twitter can be further abstracted into prototypical user networks. These networks consist of users who consistently support or oppose the Census campaign, and understanding their structure and influence is critical. By analyzing these prototypical user networks, we can better understand how different types of engagement affect the overall participation rate. This study, therefore, aims to map out these networks and evaluate their impact on the Census completion rate.

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
paragraphs: As we mentioned above, on Twitter, unlike traditional advertising such as mail, print media, or TV where the communication ends with the recipient, users are inclined to interact with others who hold similar ideologies (communities) creating homogeneity inside the community (i.e., sharing similar ideas and echoing each other) and heterogeneity across weakly connected communities (i.e., people from different communities are rarely able to find common ground with others from different network communities).

In other words, in the Census 2020 message campaign scenario, we expected to see messages from the Census Bureau itself promoting the Census, and messages sent by those who were skeptical about the Census. But the nature of social media makes it possible for other users to reiterate and amplify the message sent by the Census Bureau. Therefore, we hypothesize that three prototypical user groups will emerge from the data: Official Government Agency (GA), such as the Census Bureau; Census Skeptic (CS), namely influential users who question the legitimacy of the census; and Census Advocate (CA), namely influencers who support and wish to promote participation in the census. The controversy surrounding the Census, to which we attribute the existence of these distinct user groups, was probably intensified by the occurrence of the administration of the census and the 2020 Presidential Election, which brought into focus topics such as whether undocumented immigrants should be counted in the 2020 Census.
This leads to the following research questions about the relationship between the discussion on social media of the 2020 Census and census self-completion rates.

1. What is the relative prevalence of posts by these three prototypical user groups. 2. To what extent is self-completion directly associated with the Census social media message campaign (GA posts)? 3. Do posts by users that support self-completion (CA) strengthen the relationship between the Census campaign and self-completion (online)? 4. And are increased posts by skeptic (CS) associated with reduced self-completion (online) rates?

Therefore, we hypothesize that three prototypical user groups will emerge from the data: Official Government Agency (GA), such as the Census Bureau; Census Skeptic (CS), namely influential users who question the legitimacy of the census; and Census Advocate (CA), namely influencers who support and wish to promote participation in the census. The controversy surrounding the Census, to which we attribute the existence of these distinct user groups, was probably intensified by the occurrence of the administration of the census and the 2020 Presidential Election, which brought into focus topics such as whether undocumented immigrants should be counted in the 2020 Census.

This leads to the following research questions about the relationship between the discussion on social media of the 2020 Census and census self-completion rates.

1. What is the relative prevalence of posts by these three prototypical user groups. 2. To what extent is self-completion directly associated with the Census social media message campaign (GA posts)? 3. Do posts by users that support self-completion (CA) strengthen the relationship between the Census campaign and self-completion (online)? 4. And are increased posts by skeptic (CS) associated with reduced self-completion (online) rates?
sections.preview_head: As we mentioned above, on Twitter, unlike traditional advertising such as mail, print media, or TV where the communication ends with the recipient, users are inclined to interact with others who hold similar ideologies (communities) creating homogeneity inside the community (i.e., sharing similar ideas and echoing each other) and heterogeneity across weakly connected communities (i.e., people from different communities are rarely able to find common ground with others from different network communities).

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
paragraphs: The analysis unit is the Twitter user. The Twitter data, that is, individual posts (tweets), were sourced from a project funded by the United States Census Bureau. Data were collected with a commercial tool, Sprinklr, if they contained terms that matched a query about the US Census Bureau and its survey programs. This query, created by the US Census Bureau, was intended to select tweets that included keywords such as "census," "2020census," and "#Census2020" among many others, and did not include keywords other keywords such as "Mali," "Congo," and "Columbia" in order to filter out tweets concerning censuses in countries other than the United States. Sprinklr retrieves every "relevant" post, that is, those matching at least one keyword and not matching exclusion criteria, from several social media platforms, including Twitter, enabling us to select a corpus of tweets from January 1, 2020, to September 14, 2020, that concerned the US Census and related topics. After retrieving the data, we define the target population as Twitter users who are posting content related to these topics. The Twitter posts cover most of the 2020 Census data collection field period. According to the Census Bureau website, data collection was scheduled to end on July 31st, 2020, but due to COVID-19, a revised schedule allowed people to respond until October 15th, 2020. Our dataset contains 3,499,628 tweets in total as well as other features of tweets, such as message type (i.e., "Twitter Retweet," "Twitter Reply," "Twitter Mention," and "Twitter Update."), time, original author, and author name. 4 A user's overall Twitter network (i.e., the network created by Twitter users' actions, such as retweets, replies, mentions, and direct messages) contains different types of tweets than would comprise unique user's network and its connection. The interaction pattern within each type of network is different. For example, the intention behind retweeting often implies endorsement and agreement with the content of the original tweet (Mohammadi et al., 2018), and retweeting can reflect ideological affinity in politically oriented Twitter networks (Bode et al., 2015;Conover et al., 2021). In contrast when replying to a post, the Twitter user will comment on the message being quoted, and the comments could be positive (agree) or negative (disagree). In other words, it is likely the retweet networks (users are connected by retweeting tweets) we observe will be similar for all members of a homogenous user community. Therefore, we restricted the type of tweet we examined to "Twitter Retweet" as this type seems intimately associated with a user community. After filtering the type of tweets, we retained 1,973,913 retweets in total.

For the self-completion, we used the data published by the Census Bureau 5 , that is, the daily selfcompletion rate available from March 20th, 2020 to October 28th, 2020. We analyzed the Internet selfcompletion rate at a nationwide level because we do not have geoinformation about Twitter individual users and thus cannot analyze the effect of the tweets on self-completion at more fine-grained geographical levels. To compare the two time series, we also restricted the study period to the time window common to data sources overlapped, from March 20 th , 2020, to September 14 th , 2020.

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
paragraphs: The user interaction pattern on Twitter is complicated. Typically, influential people take the core position in a "retweet network" (i.e., the influencers express the position that is at the start of or initiates the retweet network), connecting to their followers and creating many interconnections. But there are also numerous "islands" (collections of users disconnected from the main network). To identify the communities (the groups of users who endorsed the same original tweets) in the "retweet network," we implemented the Louvain Community Detection Algorithm, which is an efficient method for decomposing networks into sub-units or communities, which are sets of highly inter-connected nodes (Blondel et al., 2008). The goal of the Louvain method is to maximize modularity iteratively by grouping nodes into different sub-units (communities), and ultimately achieve a set of communities such that the network has dense connections between the nodes within community but sparse connections between nodes in different communities. In this study, the nodes are Twitter users and the edges (connection) are retweets. Therefore, the Louvain method will in principle isolate communities where users interact more frequently than other users from other communities. Current community detection algorithms for social networks tend to generate a large number of small, isolated communities-often referred to as "islands"and a small number of large, more interconnected communities. Many of these small communities are detached from the main discussions and do not significantly contribute to the overall network structure. To address this, our study employs the method introduced by Freelon et al. (2018) specifically to filter out these isolated groups, which we consider "noise." This allows us to focus our analysis on the larger, structurally significant communities that play a crucial role in the dynamics of the network.

We constructed a network of retweet per week, used the Louvain community detection algorithm to separate each network into different communities based on their retweeting behavior, and picked the 10 largest communities. Since we restricted our study period to the weeks between March 12th and September 14th, we have 280 communities total (28 weeks × 10 communities per week). To further categorize the communities detected by the Louvain community detection algorithm, we implemented the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), a natural language processing tool widely used for topic modeling (Blei et al., 2003;Vayansky & Kumar, 2020). As an unsupervised learning algorithm, LDA can potentially reconstruct the "latent" topic behind the corpus. The statistical assumption underlying LDA is that each cluster of communities is determined (or generated) by Dirichlet distribution (with parameter α). Once we figure out the parameter, we can "predict" each community by their probability of generating by a certain cluster. In our scenario, we assume there are three latent clusters; therefore, we will obtain the probability of each community belonging to each cluster. Following Freelon and his colleagues (2018), we created a data structure analogous to a community-by-user matrix in which the columns are users, the rows are communities, and the cell value indicates the presence or absence of a user in a community. Each user is weighted by the count of their being retweeted. 6 The idea behind using a community-by-user matrix is to identify communities based on membership similarity. By replacing the document-term matrix with a community-user matrix, we are able to find the latent factor (i.e., the prototypical user group) that links the various community members.

Figure 1 illustrated this method using a simplified example for easier visualization and understanding. It depicted a retweet network comprising 26 users (represented as nodes), who are interconnected through their retweet behaviors. Note that the actual retweet network included over one million users. We then implemented the Louvain community detection algorithm on this retweet network, and therefore, we obtained 5 communities identified by the algorithm. Based on our plan, we dropped the "island" communities (all of its nodes are independent of nodes in other communities) which is community #1 in Figure 1 and only kept the 10 largest communities detected by the algorithm (which are communities #2 to #5 in our heuristic example). Next, we used LDA to identify the prototypical user groups in the remaining communities. In this scenario, the latent factor in LDA represented the prototypical user group. The figure also showed the distinction between "community" and "prototypical user": communities are constructed based on their tweeting behavior. Still, it is highly like that multiple communities share a similarity. Therefore, the prototypical user is a feature (i.e., Census advocate, skeptic, or agency) of each community. In other words, the prototypical user group is at a higher cluster level than the community. For LDA, we need to identify the number of latent factors (k). There is no gold standard for choosing k. This paper empirically tests the k range from 4 to 14. We found that all three prototypical user groups consistently appeared in each test run, that is, each value of k. As illustrated in Figure 1, in the end, using the model, we predicted each community with the probability of which prototypical user group it belongs to. We assign the prototypical user group with the highest probability of membership to each community (and users belonging to this community).

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
sections.header_raw: Relation Between User Groups and Completion Rate
sections.header_norm: relation between user groups and completion rate
paragraphs: After labeling each prototypical use group, we are able to test further the relationship between the activity (daily size of tweets) of the three prototypical user groups and, 2020 Census (online) selfcompletion rate. In particular, we aim to establish that the activity of a group caused a change in self-completing rate The Granger causality test (Granger, 1969) is a statistical hypothesis test used to determine whether one time series can predict, or "Granger cause," another time series in a given study period. Stronger than a correlation, in terms of inference validity, the Granger causality test reflects the relation between two "events" in chronological order (i.e., it compares sequentially successive pairs of events).

The Granger causality test requires that the time series involved are stationary (i.e., the value does not depend on the time at which the series is observed). Therefore, we run the Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test to see whether the time series are stationary or not. If so, we can construct a Vector Autoregressive (VAR) Model of the 2020 Census (online) self-completion rate yðtÞ, which is
where c, a 1 to a p are coefficients.
We then restrict the model by introducing a term for the lag between events in the two series, xðtÞ

Here p is the earliest lag term, and q is the latest lag term. The idea is to test whether adding the terms from another VAR model xðtÞ might add information and improve the interpretability of model yðtÞ. If the coefficients from b p to b q are equal to zero, we accept the null hypothesis that xðtÞ does not "Granger cause" yðtÞ. Otherwise, we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that xðtÞ does cause yðtÞ. In this study, the xðtÞ will be the daily size of each hypothetical user group (i.e., Census, Advocate, Government Agency, and Census Skeptic) and yðtÞ will be the daily online self-completion rate. The goal is to see whether the active daily user number "Granger causes" the daily online Census self-completion rate. If we reject the null hypothesis, we can say change in one series Granger causes the change in the other series.

In this study, by implementing the two-variables model, we assess whether the daily size of each of the three prototypical users "predicts" the online self-completion rate. If so, we can say the activity of the Twitter community "Granger causes" the 2020 Census online participation rate, that is, the activities in this social media platform affect people's propensity to participate in the 2020 Census (online). Note that "Granger causality" merely illustrates that by adding another time series xðtÞ could contribute to interpreting the variance of the original time series yðtÞ. Therefore, instead of interpreting the term as xðtÞ "cause" yðtÞ, "Granger causality" is more like the two series are "temporally related."

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
paragraphs: As introduced earlier, we apply the principles of Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) typically used in topic modeling to our social media research by constructing a user-community matrix. The frequency and interaction patterns of users within these communities guide us in determining the "topics" or what we call prototypical user groups, much as word frequency influences topic discovery in topic modeling. For example, just as a high frequency of certain words in a set of documents can indicate the prominence of a related topic, the frequent appearance of certain users within specific prototypical user groups signals their influence and the thematic focus of these groups.

This approach enables us to not only identify key influencers but also to uncover prevalent types of Twitter elites in each prototypical group. However, validating the results of LDA in this novel context poses distinct challenges. A practical method for validation is to manually inspect the activities of the most influential users identified within each prototypical user group. By analyzing their tweets and ensuring that these messages align with the characteristics of the groups they are associated with, we can confirm the face validity of our clustering. This step mirrors the process in traditional LDA of verifying whether the words frequently appearing in a topic genuinely reflect the assigned theme. Table 1 shows the ten most influential users (those who are being retweeted more frequently than others) in each prototypical user group.

Reading the clusters showed that the clustering method achieved our research purpose-the most influential users in each cluster exhibit the same perspective, more or less, toward the Census-and generated the reasonable and coherent clusters we expected to see.

For the Census Advocate group, we identified Democratic politicians and political figures such as Barack Obama, Chelsea Clinton, Kamala Harris, Andrew Yang, and Elizabeth Warren. In addition to politicians, two news agencies typically considered center or left leaning, such as NBC News and MSNBC, were also prominent. All their posts advocated for the benefits of filling out Census 2020 and encouraged immediate participation.

In the Census Skeptic group, we found Republican politicians such as Donald Trump and Marsha Blackburn, right-leaning political activists like Charlie Kirk, and conservative actors or columnists, including James Woods, Benny Johnson, and Dan Bongino. Their posts advocated for excluding "illegal immigrants" from the Census and promoted skeptical theories, such as the belief that the Census is a form of ballot harvesting for Democrats.

For the Government Agency group, in addition to the expected governmental official accounts, we observed reporters like Hansi Lo Wang, an NPR correspondent reporting on the U.S. Census, Howley Reporter, and Kyle Cheney. Regardless of their personal political leanings, their posts were focused on breaking news and updates related to the Census. Since their content was about reporting on the Census rather than expressing personal opinions, they were classified as members of the Government Agency group.

Overall, most users are reasonably categorized into the corresponding prototypical user group except for uscensusbureau and EricTrump. By looking at these "outliers," we realized that the Census Bureau connected with a wide set of users, which means the messages posted by the Census Bureau were reposted across the three groups (not limited to this particular prototypical user), making this official account less diagnostic of cluster topic. As for Eric Trump, most of his tweets are about the 2020 Presidential election (e.g., mocking Joe Biden's slips of the tongue). For example, he tweeted: "Joe has trouble with sentences and censuses. What a hot mess. Please tell me we're going to keep Joe in the basement." Even though the messages contain the keywords like Census, we cannot confidently relate the user to our research topic (the 2020 Census social media campaign). This phenomenon is what Hsieh and Murphy (2017) called "query error." So far, there is no silver bullet for eliminating this type of error; fortunately, considering the high level of overall performance, we find this amount of error to be tolerable.

To obtain an intuitive understanding of what prototypical user talked about on the Twitter platform, we selected some statements from the three groups in Table 2. 7 Based on the examples, we can see the main arguments sent by CS users are: 1. The Census is feathering seats for Democrats in Congress; 2. The Census should only count citizens (i.e., they argue a citizenship question should be added into the 2020 Census questionnaire). Those two arguments are political and ideological, especially after the US Supreme Court decision in June 2019 prohibiting the Census Bureau from asking the citizenship question on the Census questionnaire. Those types of comments may continue to affect people's likelihood of participating in the 2020 Census even though the posts were not explicitly discouraging people from filling out the Census. The influence might also be seen by how widely this type of message spread across Twitter. As for GA users, it appears more that Census updates and emphasizes the importance of filling out the census questionnaire (e.g., shape your future and funding for the local community). The CA users echo the messages sent by GA users (which will also be seen in the later time series analyses) while replying to political attack messages about the Census. However, the key points are still the messages that the Census Bureau would presumably like to deliver (funding the community, everyone should be counted, etc.), as opposed to responding to attack advertising. Those six examples provide a glimpse into range of messages spread across these prototypical user groups. At the same time, it shows the potential effect of those messages. Census is for everyone living in the US. Although not as likely to exhibit peaks as high as the CS user group, the CA users are about the same size as the Census Skeptic cluster, suggesting they are less motivated by specific topics or events. This is sensible if these tweets are driven by the flow of information from the Census Bureau as in a promotional campaign. The GA cluster is relatively small and consistently active during the study period suggesting that the CA cluster is echoing and amplifying the messages sent by the GA cluster. Those two clusters (GA and CA) seem to work in consort with each other.

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
sections.header_raw: Effect of Social Media Prototypical User Group
sections.header_norm: effect of social media prototypical user group
paragraphs: As indicated in the Methods section, in order to determine if any of these user group time series predicts the 2020 Census self-completion rate times series, that is, "Granger causes" the selfcompletion series, we need to first determine that the series are stationary and we do this by administering the ADF test to the three user group times series and the series composed of daily 2020 Census online self-completion rates. All four series are in fact stationary (p < 0:001) allowing us to run the Ganger causality test for 3 pairs of time series: daily number of users in the GA group with daily 2020 Census online self-completion rate, daily number of users in the CA group with daily 2020 Census online self-completion rate, and daily number of users in the CS group with daily 2020 Census online self-completion rate. As mentioned in the method section, the Granger causality test involves a lag term that needs to be specified before running the test. It is also worth noting that the tweets promoting Census completion, may not affect the online self-completion rate right away, and it has been shown that different lag terms significantly influence interpretation of relations between time social media and survey time series (Conrad et al., 2021). Therefore, to see whether the causality is robust to different lag terms, we tested the lag length from 1 to 5 (the unit is day). The reason this was important to do is that information updates quickly on social media platforms (especially Twitter), and it is hard to know a priori for how many days a user will be influenced by a message (e.g., whether the effect of social media message campaigns will more likely be valid within a five than three day time window). It also shows whether the Granger causality is sensitive to lag terms (i.e., the significant relation only shows for specific lag terms). Furthermore, to test whether the causality is bidirectional, we reran the Ganger causality test in a reversed order. The test results are shown in Table 3. Table 3 shows a significant Granger causality between Census Advocate user numbers and daily self-completion (online) rate. Furthermore, the Granger causality is bidirectional, that is, the number of CA users predicted the daily self-completion (online) rate and vice versa. The interpretation for this type of bidirectional Granger causality is that both factors strongly interact. In other words, the message sent from active CA users will influence the daily self-completion rate of 2020 Census, and the self-completion rate of 2020 Census will also predict the active CA user since after they finish Census online, they will be more likely post this information on Twitter to encourage other people to participate than will those who have not yet finished-or started-to complete the Census. Interestingly, compared with this finding, there is no substantial evidence to show this type of effect exists between either the daily number of active CS users or the daily number of active GA users with the self-completion (online) rate. The result does not align with the previous hypothesis, implying that the number of Census Skeptic users on Twitter will harm people's likelihood of completing the 2020 Census (online). However, after looking through the messages sent by the CS user group, it is clear that they are trying to discredit the Census rather than explicitly asking people not to complete the Census. Therefore, the Granger causality effect is not significant. As for active GA users, it implies that their activities do not directly influence people's likelihood to participate in the 2020 Census online. Instead, the messages need to be amplified by influential users in the CA group for the goals of the social media message campaign to be fulfilled.

paragraphs: This study shows that the daily number of active Twitter users in the Census Advocate prototypical user group but not the other two prototypical user groups is correlated with the selfcompletion rate of 2020 Census (online). It further suggests that the activity on social media platforms (for which we used the number of daily active users as a proxy indicator) may be a critical component of completing web surveys more generally, especially for extensive national studies. It also seems to be the case that not users have a fixed influence through their posts on the (online) completion rate: the skeptic' statements about the Census seem to have no effect on the (online) self-completion rate. However, this is not to say that effect of the messages posted by this prototypical user group is negligible. They were echoing misinformation and concerns about Census, which needed to be corrected or addressed. Although the government agencies' promotion of the Census has little effect by itself on self-completion, this should not be taken as an argument to stop promoting participation. Government Agency users just need to be sure that Census Advocate amplifies the messaging.

On the contrary, Census Advocate users amplified Government Agency messages, helping to increase the public's awareness of the Census' importance. It suggests that researchers conducting web surveys on important topics might benefit from coordinating with influential social media users and might consider recruiting different advocate who will likely reach different subgroups. In a nutshell, social media campaigns indeed become an essential component of ways to improve the participation of the target population.

This study also validates the effectiveness of the unsupervised learning method, the combination of Louvain Community Detection Algorithm and LDA. It showed the power of clustering algorithms for uncovering user networks. Firstly, it is relatively easy to obtain a social mediabased indicator (e.g., the daily number of unique active users, the daily number of unique tweets) based on the theoretical considerations such as prior knowledge of different user groups. Here, we use the daily number of active unique users as a proxy indicator of users' activities on Twitter. However, we could also imagine other valuable indicators to evaluate the impact of social media campaigns on people's behavior outside network connection (e.g., stance of their post). Besides quantitative analysis, it is also possible to dig into the details of topics in the different prototypical user groups (e.g., the content of tweets most often retweeted). It would be a helpful way for national surveys (like the American community survey, Census, etc.) to monitor public reaction to the survey project and, even more importantly, address their concerns about participating in a timely way.

Finally, our study demonstrates explicitly how Twitter and presumably other social media platforms engage various influential users in discussions of current topics, as was Census 2020 when the study was conducted. We successfully clustered users into larger, prototypical groups based on their interaction patterns. An important feature of our approach is that it uncovers hidden structure: the members of a prototypical user group may not be explicitly linked to one another, but their connection to each other can be identified and formalized through this process. This same approach can be applied irrespective of the nature of the groups or the content of their discourse.

Meanwhile, we must acknowledge that study has some the limitations. First, like we illustrated before, Granger causality cannot reveal the actual causal relationship between the social media campaign and the online participation rate, just that there is an association between two time series that can be predicted well beyond chance. The ideal approach to accounting for the causality between social media content and Census completion behavior would be to launch a random trial to observe the potential causal relations. In that case, we are able to see the improvement of participant rate between groups of people exposed to the social media message campaign and those who are not (however, in most cases, it would be time-consuming and costly.) Also, we do not have a gold standard (i.e., a corpus of tweets, each of which is labeled for the user group in which it was created) to evaluate the performance of the clustering algorithm. This is a common challenge in using unsupervised learning algorithms.

Another limitation is that our analysis only focuses on the message campaign in the Twitter platform. Besides the message campaign, Census Bureau also carried out several other campaigns, such as advertising in mass media and other social media platforms. Therefore, the increase in selfcompletion could also be influenced by other factors, which we cannot isolate from our study, other than Twitter posts from Census Advocate. Also, the users exposed to the Twitter message campaign do comprise a majority of Twitter user not to mention a majority of American residents. These coverage issues, which are inherent in using Twitter for social research (Wojcik & Hughes, 2019), limit our finding to observing the correlation between the Twitter message campaign and self-completion online rate rather than establishing the temporal causality of the former on the latter (and potentially vice versa). This is an issue that comes up in every social media research project.

Overall, based on the results we have reported here, it is safe to say the social media message campaign achieved the goal of the US Census Bureau to increase self-completion rate. However it required that an additional and loosely configured group of users, that is, Census Advocate, amplify and increase the reach of the official messages. It also seems to be the case that social media platforms (like Twitter) are becoming essential to national surveys (like the Census) to improve people's likelihood of participating. Finally, this study shows that social scientists will benefit by paying more attention to social media as a source of public opinion data and the enterprise will benefit by their applying interdisciplinary ideas and knowledge to better understand how the data generated and how this impacts its quality.

sections.paper_id: Li and Conrad - 2025 - Tracking Census Online Self-Completion Using Twitter Posts
paragraphs: Despite the encouraging results indicating that prototypical Census Skeptic do not significantly influence self-response rates, the underlying reasons for this observation remain unclear. It is uncertain whether the observed effects are due to the limited dissemination of misinformation or if people's willingness to self-complete the 2020 Census is driven predominantly by persuasive messaging from Census Advocate. Furthermore, the retweet network primarily captures agreement and homogeneity, making it challenging to discern interactions among these prototypical user groups. To address these questions, future research will focus on Twitter users' replies to patterns during the 2020 Census data collection period. Consequently, we propose extending the social network analysis to include not only retweets but also replies and quote tweets, to better understand how information is received and processed by different user groups.

Additionally, we acknowledge that the transition from Twitter to X has resulted in a shifting user base. The departure of left-leaning users complicates predictions regarding the dissemination of Census-related messages and the continued presence of the three prototypical user groups. Therefore, it is essential to monitor how this transition impacts the platform's effectiveness for these purposes. Despite this uncertainty, we are confident that our results are applicable to similar microblogging social networks, even if their relevance to the current state of Twitter (X) remains unclear. In an effort to establish robustness of our findings to changes in the social media landscape, we would like to extend our analysis to other platforms, such as Reddit or Mastodon, to address the coverage error to some extent in the future study.

1. In survey research, "coverage error" occurs when some members of the target population are not represented in the sampling frame (Groves et al., 2009). This is a common challenge in web surveys, where the reach of the survey is limited to internet users, excluding members of the population who lack internet access (e.g., Tourangeau et al., 2013). 2. https://www.census.gov/programs-surveys/decennial-census/decade/2020/planning-management/plan/ self-response.html. 3. https://www.census.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2020/2020-census-all-states-top-99-percent.html. 4. Original author is the user who created this message while author name represents the user who reposted this message. 5. https://www.census.gov/library/visualizations/interactive/2020-census-self-response-rates-map.html. 6. Network in-degree. 7. Hanzhi Lo Wang is a journalist who covers the Census Bureau; his work requires that he disseminate Census Bureau information, so it makes sense that his tweets appear in the GA cluster. 8. https://www.cnn.com/2020/07/21/politics/white-house-census-undocumented-immigrants/index.html.
